files.contentPreview:
# Annotation Scheme Design Reference / 数据标注方案设计参考

> Loaded on demand by `ai-model-lifecycle` SKILL.md when the user is designing or running annotation work.

---

# English

## 2.1 Annotation Guideline Development

The guideline is the foundation of data quality. **Finalize and team-review before annotation begins.**

The guideline document should contain:

- **Task definition**: plain-language description with examples
- **Label taxonomy**:
  - Name, definition, boundary description for every label
  - Hierarchical relationships (if any)
  - Rules for distinguishing easily confused labels (most critical part — drives consistency)
- **Annotation rules**:
  - Specific positive / negative example criteria
  - Rules for ambiguous cases (prefer false negatives vs false positives? what occlusion ratio counts as invisible?)
  - Special cases (truncated objects, tiny objects, dense overlaps)
- **Annotation examples**:
  - At least 10 positive + 5 negative + 5 edge case examples
  - Each example annotated with explanation
- **Quality requirements**:
  - Expected inter-annotator agreement (IAA)
  - Acceptance criteria

## 2.2 Annotation Tool Selection

| Task type | Recommended tools |
|-----------|------------------|
| Image classification | Label Studio, CVAT |
| Object detection / segmentation | CVAT, Labelme, Roboflow |
| OCR / text detection | PPOCRLabel, Label Studio |
| NER / text classification | Doccano, Label Studio, Prodigy |
| Multimodal / custom | Label Studio (most flex...(truncated)

files.name: data-preparation.md
files.contentPreview:
# Data Preparation Reference / 数据准备参考

> Loaded on demand by `ai-model-lifecycle` SKILL.md when the user is at the data-prep stage.

---

# English

## 1.1 Data Requirement Analysis

Before collecting any data, answer:

- **Task type**: Classification / Detection / Segmentation / OCR / NER / Generation / Recommendation / Other
- **Data modality**: Images, text, audio, video, tabular, multimodal
- **Data source**:
  - Business system logs (most realistic; may have privacy concerns)
  - Public datasets (fast start; possible domain gap)
  - Web scraping (consider legal compliance)
  - Synthetic data / augmentation (long-tail supplementation)
  - Manual collection (expensive; quality-controllable)
- **Data volume estimates** (ballpark, depends on task difficulty and model size):
  - Image classification: 500–5,000 per class
  - Object detection: 1,000–10,000 annotated instances per class
  - NER: 5,000–50,000 annotated sentences
  - LLM fine-tune: 1,000–100,000 high-quality instruction pairs

## 1.2 Data Collection Strategy

**Principle: coverage first, volume second.**

- Cover all business scenarios and edge cases first
- Mirror real production distribution as closely as possible
- Record per-class ratio if class imbalance exists (handle later in training)
- Establish data version control (DVC or similar); track every data change

**Data quality checklist:**
- [ ] Consistent formats (image size/encoding, text encoding, file naming)
- [ ] Duplicates removed
- [ ] Corrupted/inval...(truncated)

# Deployment Reference / 模型部署参考

> Loaded on demand by `ai-model-lifecycle` SKILL.md when the user is preparing to release a model.

---

# English

## 7.1 Pre-Deployment Checklist

- [ ] Model passed test set evaluation, metrics meet targets
- [ ] Model passed regression testing (Golden Set)
- [ ] Model converted to deployment format (ONNX / TensorRT / TorchScript)
- [ ] Inference latency and throughput verified on target hardware
- [ ] **Inference preprocessing aligned with training preprocessing** (common pitfall: inconsistent resize method, normalization params)
- [ ] API documentation updated
- [ ] Rollback plan ready

## 7.2 Deployment Strategy

**Canary release (recommended):**

[code block]

- Observe each stage for 1–3 days; expand only after metrics confirmed stable
- Comparison metrics: accuracy, latency, error rate, business metrics
- Set automatic rollback triggers (e.g., error rate > threshold → auto-rollback to previous version)

**A/B Testing:**
- For scenarios with clear business metrics (recommendation, search), use A/B testing to evaluate impact on business KPIs
- Random traffic allocation between experiment and control groups, sufficient sample size
- Typical experiment duration: 1–2 weeks

## 7.3 Online Monitoring

**Metrics to monitor:**

- **Model metrics**: prediction confidence distribution, per-class prediction ratio, anomalous output rate
- **System metrics**: inference latency P50/P95/P99, QPS, GPU utilization, memory usage
- **Data metrics**: inpu...(truncated)

# Multi-Round Iteration Reference / 多轮迭代闭环参考

> Loaded on demand by `ai-model-lifecycle` SKILL.md when the user is planning or executing iteration rounds.

---

# English

## 8.1 Iteration Triggers

A new iteration should be triggered when:

- Online monitoring detects metric degradation (data drift, new scenarios)
- Bad cases reported by business stakeholders accumulate beyond a threshold
- New business scenarios require model support
- Better models / methods become available
- Periodic schedule (recommend at least once every 1–3 months)

## 8.2 Standard Iteration Workflow

[code block]

## 8.3 Bad Case Analysis Methodology

Bad case analysis is the engine that drives iteration.

**Steps:**

1. **Collect**: gather error samples from production logs, user feedback, QA testing
2. **Classify**: categorize errors into:
   - Data issue (scenarios / distributions not in training set)
   - Annotation error (training labels were wrong)
   - Model capacity gap (model has seen similar samples but predicted incorrectly)
   - Preprocessing / post-processing bug (transformation caused issue)
   - Extreme edge case (very rare — may not be worth solving)
3. **Quantify**: compute proportion of each category; identify dominant type
4. **Plan**: develop strategy targeting the dominant error category
5. **Verify**: re-evaluate after optimization; confirm improvement on the targeted error type

## 8.4 Iteration Log

Record one log per iteration round. **Do not reconstruct the structure from me...(truncated)

files.name: model-selection.md
files.contentPreview:
# Model Selection Reference / 模型选型参考

> Loaded on demand by `ai-model-lifecycle` SKILL.md when the user is choosing a model.

---

# English

## 3.1 Decision Framework

Selection is not "pick the best model" — it's **"pick the most suitable model under current constraints."**

| Dimension | Considerations |
|-----------|---------------|
| Accuracy | Public benchmark performance, domain adaptability |
| Speed | Inference latency, throughput, business SLA |
| Resources | Training GPU, inference hardware, VRAM footprint |
| Maturity | Production track record, community activity, maintenance |
| Maintainability | Code quality, docs, team tech-stack alignment |
| Data efficiency | Few-shot performance, pretrained-weight availability |
| Extensibility | Ease of adding new classes / scenarios |

## 3.2 Three-Step Selection Strategy

1. **Baseline**: pick a mature, simple, quickly deployable solution to run the full pipeline first. Don't chase SOTA out of the gate.
   - Purpose: validate data quality and pipeline feasibility quickly
   - Examples: ResNet-50 for image classification, YOLOv8-m for detection, BERT-base for NER

2. **Candidates**: after baseline validation, experiment with 2–3 stronger options
   - Compare: accuracy gain vs. speed/resource cost
   - Controlled experiments: same data, same preprocessing, same evaluation protocol

3. **Production**: pick final solution based on accuracy, speed, resources, maintenance cost
   - Apply compression (distillation / pruning / qu...(truncated)

files.name: optimization-guide.md
files.contentPreview:
# Model Optimization Reference / 模型优化参考

> Loaded on demand by `ai-model-lifecycle` SKILL.md when the user is improving an existing model.

---

# English

## 5.1 Accuracy Optimization (Improving Performance)

**Optimization levers, ranked by typical priority:**

1. **Data optimization (highest priority — usually the most effective)**
   - Analyze bad cases; identify error patterns (errors concentrated in specific scenes / lighting / classes)
   - Targeted data supplementation: additional collection + re-annotation
   - Clean annotation errors: use model predictions to flag potentially mislabeled samples
   - Increase data diversity: add edge cases and hard cases

2. **Training strategy optimization**
   - Hyperparameter tuning: LR, batch size, optimizer, scheduler
   - Better augmentation combinations
   - Better loss functions (task-specific)
   - Pretrained weight selection (larger / better pretraining dataset)

3. **Model architecture optimization**
   - Stronger backbone
   - Adjust neck / head architecture
   - Add attention mechanisms
   - Model ensemble

4. **Post-processing optimization**
   - Adjust confidence thresholds
   - Optimize NMS parameters
   - Add rule-based fallback logic
   - Test-Time Augmentation (TTA)

## 5.2 Speed Optimization (Inference Acceleration)

- **Model compression**: knowledge distillation, structured pruning, unstructured pruning
- **Quantization**: FP16 / INT8 / INT4 (evaluate accuracy loss)
- **Inference frameworks**: TensorRT / ONNX Ru...(truncated)

files.name: training-guide.md
files.contentPreview:
# Model Training Reference / 模型训练参考

> Loaded on demand by `ai-model-lifecycle` SKILL.md when the user is configuring or troubleshooting training.

---

# English

## 4.1 Pre-Training Setup

**Dataset splitting:**
- Standard: Train 70% / Val 15% / Test 15%
- Principles:
  - Stratified sampling by source / time to avoid leakage
  - Roughly consistent class distribution across splits
  - Test set should approximate real production distribution
  - **Once test set is fixed, keep it unchanged across the iteration cycle** (to ensure comparability)

**Training environment:**
- Record full env info: GPU model, CUDA version, framework version, random seed
- Manage all hyperparameters via config files (no hardcoding in source)
- Recommended experiment trackers: MLflow / Weights & Biases / TensorBoard

## 4.2 Training Strategy

**Learning rate:**
- Use warmup + cosine decay or warmup + step decay
- When fine-tuning pretrained models, set backbone LR to ~1/10 of head LR
- If unsure, run a Learning Rate Finder sweep first

**Class imbalance:**
- Data level: oversample minority / undersample majority / SMOTE
- Loss level: Focal Loss / Class-Balanced Loss / Dice Loss (segmentation)
- Sampling level: Class-Balanced Sampling

**Regularization:**
- Dropout / DropPath / Label Smoothing / Weight Decay
- Data augmentation itself is a form of regularization

**Training monitoring:**
- Log per-epoch: train loss, val loss, core metrics
- Watch overfitting signals: val loss rising, train-val gap wid...(truncated)

files.name: validation-testing.md
files.contentPreview:
# Validation & Testing Reference / 模型验证与测试参考

> Loaded on demand by `ai-model-lifecycle` SKILL.md when the user is evaluating a trained model.

---

# English

## 6.1 Offline Validation

**Validation set evaluation:**
- Compute core metrics (Precision / Recall / F1 / mAP, etc., chosen by task)
- Dimensional analysis: per class, per scene, per data source
- Plot confusion matrix (classification) or PR curves (detection)
- Identify top-N bad cases for qualitative analysis

**Test set evaluation (final evaluation):**
- Test set is used **only after the model is finalized** — must not influence any tuning decision
- Compute all predefined metrics
- Compare against baseline / previous version
- Produce a complete evaluation report

## 6.2 Evaluation Report

Each iteration produces one evaluation report. **Do not reconstruct the structure from memory** — read `assets/evaluation-report-template.md` and fill it in.

The template covers:
- Basic info (version, training data, config, eval date)
- Overall metrics summary
- Dimensional analysis (by class / scene / source)
- Bad case analysis with error attribution
- Comparison with previous version
- Regression test results
- Conclusion & next-iteration direction

## 6.3 Regression Testing (Golden Set)

Every model update must include regression testing:

- Maintain a **regression test set (Golden Set)**: representative bad cases from past iterations
- Every new model must pass all Golden Set cases
- If regressions are found, analyze roo...(truncated)

# Report Writing Guide / 报告撰写指南

> Loaded on demand by `ai-project-review` SKILL.md when the user needs detailed per-section writing guidance.

---

# English

## Section 1: Requirement Analysis

Purpose: Translate business language into algorithm language.

Cover:
- Business background and objective (1–2 paragraphs, concise)
- Core algorithm task definition: decompose business need into concrete sub-tasks (e.g., object detection + OCR + post-processing rules)
- Input/output specification: data formats, sources, output forms
- Performance constraints: hard requirements on latency, throughput, accuracy
- Data status: existing volume, annotation status, quality assessment

**Writing tips**: Don't simply restate the user's requirement. Add information gain — surface hidden assumptions, flag potential ambiguities, quantify vague descriptions.

## Section 2: Algorithm Pipeline Design

Purpose: Lay out the end-to-end technical pipeline.

Cover:
- Overall architecture description (text-based data flow with input/output labels at each stage)
- Pipeline stage breakdown:
  - Data preprocessing (cleaning, augmentation, format conversion)
  - Core algorithm modules (may be serial or parallel)
  - Post-processing logic (rule filtering, confidence thresholds, NMS, fusion)
  - Output interface (API format, response schema)
- Inter-stage dependencies and data flow

**Writing tips**:
- Use Mermaid flowcharts or precise text — never vague "process it somehow"
- Every stage must specify: what g...(truncated)

---
name: api-design
description: REST API design: resource naming, HTTP methods, status codes, error shapes, versioning, pagination, idempotency
---

# API Design

Language-agnostic reference for designing HTTP APIs that are consistent, predictable, and easy to evolve.

---

## Decision Rules

Apply these by default. Deviate only when you have a concrete reason.

### Resource Naming

- Use plural nouns for collections: `/users`, `/orders`, `/invoices`
- Nest only one level deep for owned resources: `/users/{id}/addresses`
- Never nest for relationships that exist independently: use `/orders?user_id=` instead of `/users/{id}/orders/{id}/items/{id}`
- Use kebab-case for multi-word segments: `/audit-logs`, not `/auditLogs` or `/audit_logs`
- Never leak implementation details in paths: no `/getUserByEmail`, no `/db/tables/users`

### HTTP Methods

| Intent                        | Method  | Idempotent | Safe |
|-------------------------------|---------|------------|------|
| Fetch resource(s)             | GET     | Yes        | Yes  |
| Create (server assigns ID)    | POST    | No         | No   |
| Full replace                  | PUT     | Yes        | No   |
| Partial update                | PATCH   | No*        | No   |
| Delete                        | DELETE  | Yes        | No   |

*PATCH can be made idempotent with conditional requests; do so when clients will retry.

Rules:
- GET and HEAD must never mutate state
- Use POST for actions that don't map cleanly to CRUD: `/pa...(truncated)

---
name: async-queues
description: Async messaging: delivery guarantees, outbox pattern, idempotent consumers, DLQ, retries, backpressure
---

# Async & Message Queues

Language-agnostic reference for decoupling work with queues, handling delivery guarantees, and avoiding common async pitfalls.

---

## Decision Rules

### When to Use Async / a Queue

Use a queue when **any** of the following are true:
- The work does not need to be complete before the HTTP response is returned
- The work is slow and would block the request (sending email, resizing images, calling a slow third party)
- The producer and consumer need to scale independently
- You need to absorb traffic spikes without dropping work (queue acts as a buffer)
- The work must be retried on failure without the client knowing

Do not use a queue when:
- The caller needs the result immediately (use sync RPC instead)
- The system is simple and the added operational complexity is not justified
- You are trying to hide a slow synchronous operation that should actually be fixed

---

### Delivery Guarantees

Understand what your broker guarantees before designing your consumer:

| Guarantee          | Meaning                                               | Examples               |
|--------------------|-------------------------------------------------------|------------------------|
| At-most-once       | Message may be lost; never delivered twice            | UDP, fire-and-forget   |
| At-least-once      | Message will b...(truncated)

name: audit-logging
description: Audit logging: what to log, event schema, immutability, retention, actor propagation, append-only storage

# Audit Logging

Language-agnostic reference for recording who did what, when, and to which data — in a way that is trustworthy, queryable, and retained appropriately.

---

## Decision Rules

### What to Audit

Audit events where the answer to "who did this and when?" will matter:

**Always audit:**
- Authentication events: login (success and failure), logout, password change, MFA enrollment/removal
- Authorization failures: access denied to a resource
- Privilege changes: role assignments, permission grants/revocations
- Sensitive data access: viewing PII, financial records, medical data, credentials
- Mutations on sensitive resources: create, update, delete on users, accounts, payments, configuration
- Administrative actions: any action performed by an admin or service account on behalf of a user
- Data exports: any bulk data download or export

**Audit selectively (based on risk/compliance requirements):**
- High-volume read operations (can be sampled rather than fully logged)
- System-to-system calls in internal services

**Do not audit:**
- Health check endpoints
- Static asset requests
- High-volume low-sensitivity reads where the storage cost outweighs the value

---

### What Each Audit Event Must Contain

Every audit event is immutable after creation. Include enough context to reconstruct what happened without referencin...(truncated)

# Authentication & Authorization

Language-agnostic reference for verifying identity (AuthN) and enforcing permissions (AuthZ) correctly and safely.

---

## Decision Rules

### Authentication vs. Authorization

- **Authentication (AuthN):** Who are you? Verify identity. Runs first.
- **Authorization (AuthZ):** What are you allowed to do? Enforce permissions. Runs after AuthN.
- Never conflate them. A request can be authenticated but not authorized (valid token, wrong permissions).

---

### Choosing an Authentication Mechanism

| Mechanism         | Best for                                               | Avoid when                                  |
|-------------------|--------------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Session + cookie  | Browser-based apps, server-rendered UIs                | APIs consumed by non-browser clients        |
| JWT (stateless)   | Stateless APIs, microservices, mobile clients          | You need instant revocation without a store |
| API key           | Server-to-server, developer/third-party API consumers  | User-facing login flows                     |
| OAuth 2.0         | Delegated access, third-party integrations             | Internal service-to-service (use mTLS/keys) |
| mTLS              | Internal service-to-service in zero-trust networks    ...(truncated)

name: background-jobs
description: Background jobs and scheduling: idempotency, retries, cron locking, zombie detection, observability
---

# Background Jobs & Scheduling

Language-agnostic reference for running deferred work, periodic tasks, and long-running jobs reliably.

---

## Decision Rules

### When to Use a Background Job

Use a background job when:
- The work does not need to complete before the HTTP response is returned
- The work is slow enough to hurt request latency (> ~200ms as a rule of thumb)
- The work must be retried independently of the original request
- The work is triggered on a schedule rather than a user action

Do not use a background job when:
- The result is needed immediately by the caller (use sync processing)
- The work is trivial and adding a job queue introduces unnecessary complexity

---

### Job Queue vs. Cron Scheduler

| Trigger         | Use                              | Examples                          |
|-----------------|----------------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| Event-driven    | Job queue (enqueued on demand)   | Send email, process upload, charge card |
| Time-driven     | Cron / scheduler                 | Daily report, hourly sync, cleanup old records |

Many systems need both. A common pattern: the scheduler enqueues a job at the scheduled time; the job queue processes it. This separates scheduling concerns from execution concerns and gives you retries, observability, and concurrency control ...(truncated)

# Caching

Language-agnostic reference for deciding when to cache, which strategy to use, and how to avoid correctness traps.

---

## Decision Rules

### Should You Cache This?

Cache when **all** of the following are true:
- The data is read more often than it changes
- The computation or query to produce it is measurably expensive
- Serving slightly stale data is acceptable, OR you have a reliable invalidation strategy

Do not cache when:
- The data must always be fresh (account balance at point of transaction, auth token validity)
- The dataset is so large that cache hit rate will be too low to justify the overhead
- The system is not yet slow — do not cache speculatively

Ask before caching: "What is the worst case if this data is stale?" If the answer is "incorrect financial data" or "security bypass," do not cache without an invalidation strategy.

---

### Caching Layers

Apply caches closest to the consumer first:

| Layer               | Examples                            | Latency    | Scope          |
|---------------------|-------------------------------------|------------|----------------|
| In-process (local)  | In-memory map, LRU cache in app     | ~nanoseconds | Single instance |
| Shared / distributed| Redis, Memcached                    | ~1ms       | All instances  |
| HTTP / CDN cache    | Varnish, Cloudflare, browser cac...(truncated)

name: data-modeling
description: Data modeling: schema design, normalization, indexing, primary keys, soft deletes, schema evolution
---

# Data Modeling

Language-agnostic reference for designing schemas that are correct, queryable, and safe to evolve.

---

## Decision Rules

### Normalization vs. Denormalization

Normalize by default (3NF). Denormalize only when you have a measured query performance problem.

**Normalize when:**
- Data is written frequently
- Consistency is critical (financial records, inventory)
- Query patterns are not yet known

**Denormalize when:**
- A query joins more than 4–5 tables and is on the critical path
- The data is read-heavy and rarely updated
- You have a measured latency problem, not a hypothetical one

Never denormalize to avoid learning SQL joins.

### Choosing a Primary Key

- Use surrogate keys (system-generated IDs) as primary keys — not natural keys
- Prefer UUIDs (v4 or v7) over auto-increment integers for keys exposed to clients:
  - No enumeration attack surface
  - Safe to generate client-side
  - Works across distributed systems
- Use UUID v7 (time-ordered) over v4 when index locality matters (most relational DBs)
- Keep auto-increment integers as internal-only PKs only if you have a strong performance reason and never expose them in APIs

### Natural Keys

- Natural keys (email, SSN, username) change in the real world — never use them as PKs
- They are fine as unique constraints alongside a surrogate PK
- Composite natura...(truncated)

name: db-access-patterns
description: Database access patterns: connection pooling, transactions, N+1 queries, locking, bulk operations, read replicas

# Database Access Patterns

Language-agnostic reference for writing database interactions that are correct, efficient, and safe under load.

---

## Decision Rules

### Connection Management

- Never open a new connection per request — use a connection pool
- Size the pool to match the DB server's capacity, not the application's concurrency:
  - A good starting formula: `pool_size = num_cores * 2 + effective_spindle_count` (Hikari/PgBouncer rule of thumb)
  - A pool that is too large causes DB-side contention; too small causes queuing in the app
- Set a connection acquisition timeout — fail fast rather than queue indefinitely
- Set a max connection lifetime to recycle connections and avoid stale state
- In serverless / short-lived environments, use an external pooler (PgBouncer, RDS Proxy) — do not rely on in-process pools

Connection pool settings to always configure explicitly:

| Setting                  | Purpose                                              |
|--------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|
| `min_pool_size`          | Warm connections kept alive                          |
| `max_pool_size`          | Hard ceiling on DB connections                       |
| `acquire_timeout`        | How long to wait for a connection before erroring    |
| `max_lifetime`          ...(truncated)

name: db-migrations
description: Database migrations: expand/contract, safe vs unsafe operations, zero-downtime, large table patterns
---

# Database Migrations

Language-agnostic reference for evolving schemas safely, without downtime or data loss.

---

## Decision Rules

### Core Principles

- **Migrations are code.** Store them in version control alongside the application code that depends on them.
- **Migrations are append-only.** Never edit a migration that has already run in any environment. Add a new migration to fix it.
- **Migrations run forward only in production.** Design rollback at the application level (expand/contract), not by running `DOWN` migrations on a live system.
- **Decouple schema changes from code deploys.** The schema must be compatible with both the old and new version of the application during a rolling deploy.

---

### The Expand / Contract Pattern

The only safe way to make breaking schema changes without downtime. Every breaking change is a three-phase process:

**Phase 1 — Expand:**
- Add the new column/table/index alongside the old one
- New code writes to both old and new; reads from old
- Old code still works (new column is nullable or has a default)
- Deploy this phase; both old and new app versions are compatible

**Phase 2 — Migrate (Backfill):**
- Populate the new column/table with data from the old one
- Run as a background job or a migration with batched updates
- Verify data is complete and correct before proceeding

**Phase 3 —...(truncated)

# Error Handling & Observability

Language-agnostic reference for making backend systems debuggable, alertable, and operationally transparent.

---

## Decision Rules

### Error Handling

**Classify errors before handling them:**

| Class              | Cause                             | Right response                          |
|--------------------|-----------------------------------|-----------------------------------------|
| Validation error   | Bad client input                  | 4xx, return details, do not log as error|
| Business rule error| Valid input, rejected by logic    | 4xx, return reason, do not log as error |
| Not found          | Resource does not exist           | 404, do not log as error                |
| Auth failure       | Missing/invalid credentials       | 401/403, log at info level              |
| Dependency failure | Upstream DB/service unavailable   | 503/502, log as error with context      |
| Programming error  | Bug, unexpected state             | 500, log as error with full context     |

Rules:
- Do not log 4xx errors as errors — they are client mistakes, not service failures. Logging them as errors pollutes on-call alerts.
- Do log 5xx errors as errors with full context — they are your service's fault.
- Never swallow errors silently (`catch (e) {}`). Either handle, ...(truncated)

# Input Validation & Injection Prevention

Language-agnostic reference for rejecting malicious input and preventing injection attacks across SQL, HTML, shell, and other interpreters.

---

## Decision Rules

### Validate All Input at the Boundary

Treat every value that enters the system from outside as untrusted until validated:
- HTTP request bodies, query parameters, headers, cookies
- File uploads (name, type, content)
- Data read from a queue or event stream published by an external system
- Webhook payloads (even signed ones — validate the structure after verifying the signature)
- Data returned from third-party APIs

**Validate as early as possible** — at the API boundary, before the value reaches any business logic or data layer. Reject invalid input with a clear 400/422 error; do not attempt to sanitize and proceed.

**Validation checks to apply to every input:**
- **Type:** is the value the expected type (string, integer, boolean)?
- **Presence:** is the value present if required?
- **Length / size:** string length, array count, file size within allowed bounds?
- **Format:** matches expected pattern (email, UUID, ISO date, phone number)?
- **Range:** numeric value within allowed min/max?
- **Enum membership:** value is one of the allowed set?
- **Encoding:** valid UTF-8 or expected encoding?

---

### SQL Injection

**The ...(truncated)

# Pagination & Filtering at Scale

Language-agnostic reference for building list endpoints that stay fast, stable, and correct as data grows.

---

## Decision Rules

### Choose the Right Pagination Method

| Method          | How it works                                      | Best for                                      |
|-----------------|---------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------|
| Offset          | `LIMIT n OFFSET m`                                | Small datasets, UIs needing page numbers      |
| Keyset (cursor) | `WHERE (created_at, id) < (?, ?)  ORDER BY ... LIMIT n` | Large datasets, feeds, real-time data    |
| Cursor (opaque) | Encode keyset values in a base64 token            | Public APIs hiding implementation details     |
| Seek (composite)| Keyset on multiple columns for stable sort        | Multi-column sort with high correctness needs |

**Default to keyset pagination for any table that will grow large.** Offset pagination degrades linearly — `OFFSET 100000` causes the DB to scan and discard 100,000 rows on every request.

Use offset only when:
- The dataset is small and bounded (reference data, config)
- The UI genuinely requires random page access by number
- Total count is required and performance is acceptable

---

### Offset Pagination Problems

*...(truncated)

name: payment-integration
description: Payment integration: PCI scope, tokenization, idempotency keys, webhook handling, reconciliation, disputes
---

# Payment Integration

Language-agnostic reference for integrating with payment providers (Stripe, Braintree, Adyen, etc.) correctly, safely, and reliably.

---

## Decision Rules

### Never Touch Raw Card Data

- Do not accept, transmit, or store raw card numbers (PANs), CVVs, or full magnetic stripe data on your servers
- Use the provider's hosted UI components (Stripe Elements, Braintree Drop-in, Adyen Web Components) to collect card data directly in the client — the data goes to the provider, not your server
- Your server receives only a token or payment method ID representing the card
- This is the primary mechanism for reducing PCI DSS scope — if raw card data never touches your infrastructure, you qualify for the simplest compliance tier (SAQ A)
- Never log, store, or pass card numbers through your backend even "temporarily"

---

### Authorization vs. Capture

Most providers separate the charge into two steps:

- **Authorize (auth):** Verifies the card and reserves the funds. No money moves.
- **Capture:** Settles the authorized amount. Money moves.

Rules:
- Use auth + capture when the final amount is not known at authorization time (e.g., hotel holds, marketplaces)
- Capture within the provider's authorization window (typically 7 days for Stripe; varies by provider and card network) — uncaptured auths expire and t...(truncated)

# Rate Limiting & Idempotency

Language-agnostic reference for protecting services from overload and making mutations safe to retry.

---

## Decision Rules

### Rate Limiting

**Apply rate limits at every public-facing entry point.** Internal service-to-service calls may be exempt if the caller is trusted and controlled.

**Choose the limit scope:**

| Scope               | When to use                                              |
|---------------------|----------------------------------------------------------|
| Per IP              | Unauthenticated endpoints, login, signup                 |
| Per API key / token | Authenticated API consumers with known quotas            |
| Per user            | User-facing actions (posts, uploads, searches)           |
| Per tenant          | Multi-tenant systems with per-tenant SLAs                |
| Global              | Last-resort protection; use alongside scoped limits      |

Use the most specific scope that fits. Per-IP limits alone are weak (shared NAT, proxies). Per-user or per-key limits are more accurate.

**Choose the right algorithm:**

| Algorithm          | How it works                                         | Best for                              |
|--------------------|------------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------|
| Fi...(truncated)

name: secrets-management
description: Secrets management: secret storage, rotation, least privilege, secret scanning, encryption keys
---

# Secrets Management

Language-agnostic reference for storing, distributing, and rotating secrets without leaking them.

---

## Decision Rules

### What Is a Secret

A secret is any value that grants access or proves identity:
- Database credentials (host, user, password, connection string)
- API keys and tokens (third-party services, internal service accounts)
- Signing keys and encryption keys
- OAuth client secrets
- Private TLS certificates and keys
- Webhook signing secrets

Configuration that is not a secret: feature flags, non-sensitive URLs, log levels, timeouts. These can live in environment config or a config file.

---

### Where Secrets Must Never Live

- **Source code** — committed secrets persist in git history even after deletion; rotate immediately if this happens
- **Build artifacts** — Docker images, compiled binaries, deployment packages
- **Client-side code** — browser JavaScript, mobile apps (trivially extracted)
- **Unencrypted config files** committed to version control (`.env`, `config.yaml`, `application.properties`)
- **Application logs** — see [error-handling-observability](../error-handling-observability/README.md)
- **URLs** — secrets in query strings appear in access logs, browser history, and referrer headers
- **Environment variables in CI that are printed** — mask secrets in CI output; never echo them
...(truncated)

# Service Boundaries

Language-agnostic reference for deciding when to split services, where to draw boundaries, and how services should communicate.

---

## Decision Rules

### When to Split a Service

Start with a monolith. Split only when you have a concrete, observable problem that splitting solves.

**Valid reasons to split:**
- Independent deployment is required (different release cadences, different teams owning different parts)
- Independent scaling is required (one component is the bottleneck and cannot scale as part of the whole)
- A component has meaningfully different operational characteristics (CPU-bound ML inference vs. I/O-bound API)
- Regulatory or security isolation is required (PCI scope reduction, data residency)
- The monolith's build/test/deploy cycle has become the bottleneck for team productivity

**Invalid reasons to split:**
- "Microservices are best practice" — they are a tradeoff, not a default
- The monolith is slow — slow monoliths are usually a code or DB problem, not an architecture problem
- Team preference for the technology — this is a reason to isolate a library, not a service
- Perceived cleanliness — distributed systems are harder to operate, debug, and keep consistent than a well-structured monolith

Rule of thumb: **if you are not sure whether to split, don't.** The cost of a prematur...(truncated)

# Frontend Code Review

## Intent
Use this skill whenever the user asks to review frontend code (especially `.tsx`, `.ts`, or `.js` files). Support two review modes:

1. **Pending-change review** – inspect staged/working-tree files slated for commit and flag checklist violations before submission.
2. **File-targeted review** – review the specific file(s) the user names and report the relevant checklist findings.

Stick to the checklist below for every applicable file and mode.

## Checklist
See [references/code-quality.md](references/code-quality.md), [references/performance.md](references/performance.md) for the living checklist split by category—treat it as the canonical set of rules to follow.

Flag each rule violation with urgency metadata so future reviewers can prioritize fixes.

## Review Process
1. Open the relevant component/module. Gather lines that relate to class names, React Flow hooks, prop memoization, and styling.
2. For each rule in the review point, note where the code deviates and capture a representative snippet.
3. Compose the review section per the template below. Group violations first by **Urgent** flag, then by category order (Code Quality, Performance).

## Required output
When invoked, the response must exactly follow...(truncated)

## Conditional class names use utility function

IsUrgent: True
Category: Code Quality

### Description

Ensure conditional CSS is handled via the shared `classNames` instead of custom ternaries, string concatenation, or template strings. Centralizing class logic keeps components consistent and easier to maintain.

## Tailwind-first styling

IsUrgent: True
Category: Code Quality

### Description

Favor Tailwind CSS utility classes instead of adding new `.module.css` files unless a Tailwind combination cannot achieve the required styling. Keeping styles in Tailwind improves consistency and reduces maintenance overhead.

Update this file when adding, editing, or removing Code Quality rules so the catalog remains accurate.

## Classname ordering for easy overrides

### Description

When writing components, always place the incoming `className` prop after the component’s own class values so that downstream consumers can override or extend the styling. This keeps your component’s defaults but still lets external callers change or remove specific styles.

## React Flow data usage

Category: Performance

### Description

When rendering React Flow, prefer `useNodes`/`useEdges` for UI consumption and rely on `useStoreApi` inside callbacks that mutate or read node/edge state. Avoid manually pulling Flow data outside of these hooks.

# Data Patterns

Choose the right data fetching pattern for each use case.

## Decision Tree

[code block]

## Pattern 1: Server Components (Preferred for Reads)

Fetch data directly in Server Components - no API layer needed.

[code block]

**Benefits**:
- No API to maintain
- No client-server waterfall
- Secrets stay on server
- Direct database access

## Pattern 2: Server Actions (Preferred for Mutations)

Server Actions are the recommended way to handle mutations.

[code block]

[code block]

**Benefits**:
- End-to-end type safety
- Progressive enhancement (works without JS)
- Automatic request handling
- Integrated with React transitions

**Constraints**:
- POST only (no GET caching semantics)
- Internal use only (no external access)
- Cannot return non-serializable data

## Pattern 3: Route Handlers (APIs)

# Directives

## React Directives

These are React directives, not Next.js specific.

### `'use client'`

Marks a component as a Client Component. Required for:
- React hooks (`useState`, `useEffect`, etc.)
- Event handlers (`onClick`, `onChange`)
- Browser APIs (`window`, `localStorage`)

[code block]

Reference: https://react.dev/reference/rsc/use-client

### `'use server'`

Marks a function as a Server Action. Can be passed to Client Components.

[code block]

Or inline within a Server Component:

[code block]

Reference: https://react.dev/reference/rsc/use-server

---

## Next.js Directive

### `'use cache'`

Marks a function or component for caching. Part of Next.js Cache Components.

[code block]

Requires `cacheComponents: true` in `next.config.ts`.

For detailed usage including cache profiles, `cacheLife()`, `cacheTag()`, and `updateTag()`, see the `next-cache-components` skill.

# Parallel & Intercepting Routes

Parallel routes render multiple pages in the same layout. Intercepting routes show a different UI when navigating from within your app vs direct URL access. Together they enable modal patterns.

## File Structure

[code block]

## Step 1: Root Layout with Slot

[code block]

## Step 2: Default File (Critical!)

**Every parallel route slot MUST have a `default.tsx`** to prevent 404s on hard navigation.

[code block]

Without this file, refreshing any page will 404 because Next.js can't determine what to render in the `@modal` slot.

## Step 3: Intercepting Route (Modal)

The `(.)` prefix intercepts routes at the same level.

[code block]

## Step 4: Full Page (Direct Access)

[code block]

## Step 5: Modal Component with Correct Closing

**Critical: Use `router.back()` to close modals, NOT `router.push()` or `<Link>`.**

[code block]

### Why NOT `router.push('/')` or `<Link href="/">`?

Using `push` or `Link` to "close" a modal:
1. Adds a new history entry (back button shows modal again)
2. Doesn't properly clear the intercepted route
3. Can cause the modal to flash or persist unexpectedly

`router.back()` correctly:
1. Removes the intercepted route from history
2. Returns to the previous page
3. Properly unmounts the modal

## Route Matcher Reference

Matchers match **route segments**, not filesystem paths:

| Matcher | Matches | Example |
|---------|---------|---------|
| `(.)` | Same level | `@modal/(.)photos` intercepts `/photos` |
| `(.....(truncated)

If you need both a page and an API at the same path, use different paths:

## Environment Behavior

Route handlers run in a **Server Component-like environment**:

- Yes: Can use `async/await`
- Yes: Can access `cookies()`, `headers()`
- Yes: Can use Node.js APIs
- No: Cannot use React hooks
- No: Cannot use React DOM APIs
- No: Cannot use browser APIs

## Request Helpers

[code block]

## Response Helpers

[code block]

## When to Use Route Handlers vs Server Actions

| Use Case | Route Handlers | Server Actions |
|----------|----------------|----------------|
| Form submissions | No | Yes |
| Data mutations from UI | No | Yes |
| Third-party webhooks | Yes | No |
| External API consumption | Yes | No |
| Public REST API | Yes | No |
| File uploads | Both work | Both work |

**Prefer Server Actions** for mutations triggered from your UI.
**Use Route Handlers** for external integrations and public APIs.

files.name: rsc-boundaries.md
files.contentPreview:
# RSC Boundaries

Detect and prevent invalid patterns when crossing Server/Client component boundaries.

## Detection Rules

### 1. Async Client Components Are Invalid

Client components **cannot** be async functions. Only Server Components can be async.

**Detect:** File has `'use client'` AND component is `async function` or returns `Promise`

[code block]

[code block]

### 2. Non-Serializable Props to Client Components

Props passed from Server → Client must be JSON-serializable.

**Detect:** Server component passes these to a client component:
- Functions (except Server Actions with `'use server'`)
- `Date` objects
- `Map`, `Set`, `WeakMap`, `WeakSet`
- Class instances
- `Symbol` (unless globally registered)
- Circular references

[code block]

[code block]

[code block]

[code block]

### 3. Server Actions Are the Exception

Functions marked with `'use server'` CAN be passed to client components.

[code block]

## Quick Reference

| Pattern | Valid? | Fix |
|---------|--------|-----|
| `'use client'` + `async function` | No | Fetch in server parent, pass data |
| Pass `() => {}` to client | No | Define in client or use server action |
| Pass `new Date()` to client | No | Use `.toISOString()` |
| Pass `new Map()` to client | No | Convert to object/array |
| Pass class instance to client | No | Pass plain object |
| Pass server action to client | Yes | - |
| Pass `string/number/boolean` | Yes | - |
| Pass plain object/array | Yes | - |

files.name: runtime-selection.md
files.contentPreview:
# Runtime Selection

## Use Node.js Runtime by Default

Use the default Node.js runtime for new routes and pages. Only use Edge runtime if the project already uses it or there's a specific requirement.

[code block]

## When to Use Each

### Node.js Runtime (Default)

- Full Node.js API support
- File system access (`fs`)
- Full `crypto` support
- Database connections
- Most npm packages work

### Edge Runtime

- Only for specific edge-location latency requirements
- Limited API (no `fs`, limited `crypto`)
- Smaller cold start
- Geographic distribution needs

## Detection

**Before adding `runtime = 'edge'`**, check:
1. Does the project already use Edge runtime?
2. Is there a specific latency requirement?
3. Are all dependencies Edge-compatible?

If unsure, use Node.js runtime.

## ISR and Cache Handlers

### The Problem

ISR (Incremental Static Regeneration) uses filesystem caching by default. This **breaks with multiple instances**:

- Instance A regenerates page → saves to its local disk
- Instance B serves stale page → doesn't see Instance A's cache
- Load balancer sends users to random instances → inconsistent content

### Solution: Custom Cache Handler

Next.js 14+ supports custom cache handlers for shared storage:

[code block]

#### Redis Cache Handler Example

[code block]

[code block]

## What Works vs What Needs Setup

| Feature | Single Instance | Multi-Instance | Notes |
|---------|----------------|----------------|-------|
| SSR | Yes | Yes | No special setup |
| SSG | Yes | Yes | Built at deploy time |
| ISR | Yes | Needs cache handler | Filesystem cache breaks |
| Image Optimization | Yes | Yes | CPU-intensive, consider CDN |
| Middleware | Yes | Yes | Runs on Node.js |
| Edge Runtime | Limited | Limited | Some features Node-only |

files.name: react-performance-guidelines.md
files.contentPreview:
# React Best Practices - Complete Document

**Version 0.1.0**
Vercel Engineering
January 2026

> **Note:**
> This document is mainly for agents and LLMs to follow when maintaining, generating, or refactoring React and Next.js codebases at Vercel.

---

## Abstract

Comprehensive performance optimization guide for React and Next.js applications, designed for AI agents and LLMs. Contains 40+ rules across 8 categories, prioritized by impact from critical (eliminating waterfalls, reducing bundle size) to incremental (advanced patterns). Each rule includes detailed explanations, real-world examples comparing incorrect vs. correct implementations, and specific impact metrics to guide automated refactoring and code generation.

---

## Table of Contents

files.name: _sections.md
files.contentPreview:
# Sections

This file defines all sections, their ordering, impact levels, and descriptions.
The section ID (in parentheses) is the filename prefix used to group rules.

---

## 1. Eliminating Waterfalls (async)

**Impact:** CRITICAL
**Description:** Waterfalls are the #1 performance killer. Each sequential await adds full network latency. Eliminating them yields the largest gains.

## 2. Bundle Size Optimization (bundle)

**Impact:** CRITICAL
**Description:** Reducing initial bundle size improves Time to Interactive and Largest Contentful Paint.

## 3. Server-Side Performance (server)

**Impact:** HIGH
**Description:** Optimizing server-side rendering and data fetching eliminates server-side waterfalls and reduces response times.

## 4. Client-Side Data Fetching (client)

**Impact:** MEDIUM-HIGH
**Description:** Automatic deduplication and efficient data fetching patterns reduce redundant network requests.

## 5. Re-render Optimization (rerender)

**Impact:** MEDIUM
**Description:** Reducing unnecessary re-renders minimizes wasted computation and improves UI responsiveness.

## 6. Rendering Performance (rendering)

**Impact:** MEDIUM
**Description:** Optimizing the rendering process reduces the work the browser needs to do.

## 7. JavaScript Performance (js)

**Impact:** LOW-MEDIUM
**Description:** Micro-optimizations for hot paths can add up to meaningful improvements.

## 8. Advanced Patterns (advanced)

**Impact:** LOW
**Description:** Advanced patterns for specific cases...(truncated)

files.name: async-suspense-boundaries.md
files.contentPreview:
---
title: Strategic Suspense Boundaries
impact: HIGH
impactDescription: faster initial paint
tags: async, suspense, streaming, layout-shift
---

## Strategic Suspense Boundaries

Instead of awaiting data in async components before returning JSX, use Suspense boundaries to show the wrapper UI faster while data loads.

**Incorrect (wrapper blocked by data fetching):**

[code block]

The entire layout waits for data even though only the middle section needs it.

**Correct (wrapper shows immediately, data streams in):**

[code block]

Sidebar, Header, and Footer render immediately. Only DataDisplay waits for data.

**When NOT to use this pattern:**

- Critical data needed for layout decisions (affects positioning)
- SEO-critical content above the fold
- Small, fast queries where suspense overhead isn't worth it
- When you want to avoid layout shift (loading → content jump)

**Trade-off:** Faster initial paint vs potential layout shift. Choose based on your UX priorities.

files.name: bundle-barrel-imports.md
files.contentPreview:
---
title: Avoid Barrel File Imports
impact: CRITICAL
impactDescription: 200-800ms import cost, slow builds
tags: bundle, imports, tree-shaking, barrel-files, performance
---

## Avoid Barrel File Imports

Import directly from source files instead of barrel files to avoid loading thousands of unused modules. **Barrel files** are entry points that re-export multiple modules (e.g., `index.js` that does `export * from './module'`).

Popular icon and component libraries can have **up to 10,000 re-exports** in their entry file. For many React packages, **it takes 200-800ms just to import them**, affecting both development speed and production cold starts.

**Why tree-shaking doesn't help:** When a library is marked as external (not bundled), the bundler can't optimize it. If you bundle it to enable tree-shaking, builds become substantially slower analyzing the entire module graph.

**Incorrect (imports entire library):**

[code block]

**Correct (imports only what you need):**

[code block]

**Alternative (Next.js 13.5+):**

[code block]

Direct imports provide 15-70% faster dev boot, 28% faster builds, 40% faster cold starts, and significantly faster HMR.

Libraries commonly affected: `lucide-react`, `@mui/material`, `@mui/icons-material`, `@tabler/icons-react`, `react-icons`, `@headlessui/react`, `@radix-ui/react-*`, `lodash`, `ramda`, `date-fns`, `rxjs`, `react-use`.

Reference: [How we optimized package imports in Next.js](https://vercel.com/blog/how-we-optimized-package-impor...(truncated)

files.name: js-length-check-first.md
files.contentPreview:
---
title: Early Length Check for Array Comparisons
impact: HIGH
impactDescription: avoids expensive operations when lengths differ
tags: javascript, arrays, performance, optimization, comparison
---

## Early Length Check for Array Comparisons

When comparing arrays with expensive operations (sorting, deep equality, serialization), check lengths first. If lengths differ, the arrays cannot be equal.

In real-world applications, this optimization is especially valuable when the comparison runs in hot paths (event handlers, render loops).

**Incorrect (always runs expensive comparison):**

[code block]

Two O(n log n) sorts run even when `current.length` is 5 and `original.length` is 100. There is also overhead of joining the arrays and comparing the strings.

**Correct (O(1) length check first):**

[code block]

This new approach is more efficient because:
- It avoids the overhead of sorting and joining the arrays when lengths differ
- It avoids consuming memory for the joined strings (especially important for large arrays)
- It avoids mutating the original arrays
- It returns early when a difference is found

## Use toSorted() Instead of sort() for Immutability

`.sort()` mutates the array in place, which can cause bugs with React state and props. Use `.toSorted()` to create a new sorted array without mutation.

**Incorrect (mutates original array):**

[code block]

**Correct (creates new array):**

[code block]

**Why this matters in React:**

1. **Props/state mutations break React's immutability model** - React expects props and state to be treated as read-only
2. **Causes stale closure bugs** - Mutating arrays inside closures (callbacks, effects) can lead to unexpected behavior

**Browser support:**

`.toSorted()` is available in all modern browsers (Chrome 110+, Safari 16+, Firefox 115+, Node.js 20+). For older environments, use spread operator:

[code block]

**Other immutable array methods:**

- `.toSorted()` - immutable sort
- `.toReversed()` - immutable reverse
- `.toSpliced()` - immutable splice
- `.with()` - immutable element replacement

## Prevent Hydration Mismatch Without Flickering

When rendering content that depends on client-side storage (localStorage, cookies), avoid both SSR breakage and post-hydration flickering by injecting a synchronous script that updates the DOM before React hydrates.

**Incorrect (breaks SSR):**

[code block]

Server-side rendering will fail because `localStorage` is undefined.

**Incorrect (visual flickering):**

[code block]

Component first renders with default value (`light`), then updates after hydration, causing a visible flash of incorrect content.

**Correct (no flicker, no hydration mismatch):**

[code block]

The inline script executes synchronously before showing the element, ensuring the DOM already has the correct value. No flickering, no hydration mismatch.

This pattern is especially useful for theme toggles, user preferences, authentication states, and any client-only data that should render immediately without flashing default values.

## Cross-Request LRU Caching

`React.cache()` only works within one request. For data shared across sequential requests (user clicks button A then button B), use an LRU cache.

# Android Adaptation Standards

## Android Version Support

- Support **Android 7.0 (API 24) as the minimum** for new projects unless the client brief specifies otherwise — this covers 98%+ of active Android devices
- Use `@RequiresApi` and runtime `Build.VERSION.SDK_INT` checks for newer APIs:
  [code block]
- Never suppress `NewApi` lint warnings without a surrounding version check

## Screen Size Adaptation

Android devices range from 4" phones to 13" tablets and foldables. Do not assume a phone form factor.

- Use **WindowSizeClass** to adapt layouts to available space:
  [code block]
- Test on at minimum: **Pixel 4a (5.8")**, **Pixel 7 (6.3")**, **Pixel Tablet (10.95")**, and the **Foldable emulator**
- Use `dp` units for all dimensions — never `px`
- Layouts must not break or overflow on 360dp minimum width devices

## Foldable Support

- Use `WindowManager Jetpack` library to detect fold state:
  [code block]
- Do not assume the fold is always vertical — detect orientation from `FoldingFeature.orientation`
- Test the **tabletop posture** (horizontal fold) for media and camera experiences

## Dark Theme

- All colors mus...(truncated)

# Android Background Processing Standards

Background processing in Android is uniquely complex because the OS aggressively restricts background work to preserve battery. The restrictions differ significantly across Android versions (6, 8, 12, 14). Choosing the wrong mechanism causes missed jobs, battery drain, or ANRs.

## Choosing the Right Mechanism

| Scenario | Mechanism |
|---|---|
| Short async work tied to a screen | `viewModelScope` coroutine |
| Deferrable work that must complete (upload, sync) | `WorkManager` |
| User-visible ongoing operation (music, download) | Foreground Service |
| Exact alarm (calendar reminder) | `AlarmManager` with exact alarm permission |
| Push-triggered background processing | FCM + WorkManager |
| Periodic background sync | `WorkManager` periodic work |

**Never use `Service` for background work that isn't user-visible.** The OS kills background services on Android 8+ with no warning. Use WorkManager instead.

## WorkManager — The Standard for Deferrable Work

WorkManager is the correct tool for any work that:
- Must comple...(truncated)

# Android Component Rules

Jetpack Compose is the default UI framework for all new 57Blocks Android projects. These rules define the responsibility boundaries between UI and ViewModel layers with Compose as the primary model. XML Views are covered at the end for legacy project support.

## ViewModel Responsibilities

Every screen has exactly one ViewModel. The ViewModel:
- Is the single source of truth for screen state — exposes one `uiState: StateFlow<ScreenUiState>`
- Contains all business logic, input validation, and navigation decisions
- Calls UseCases or Repositories — never other ViewModels
- Survives configuration changes — **never holds references to Views, Composables, Fragments, or Context** (use `@ApplicationContext` if Context is needed)
- Uses `viewModelScope` for all coroutines — cancelled automatically when the ViewModel is cleared

[code block]

| `viewModelScope` | Coroutines tied to ViewModel lifecycle — cancelled on `onCleared()` |
| `lifecycleScope` | Coroutines tied to Activity/Fragment lifecycle |
| `repeatOnLifecycle` | Collecting Flows safely — pauses collection in background |
| `rememberCoroutineScope` | Launching coroutines from Composable event handlers |
| Custom `CoroutineScope` | Long-running operations outliving any single screen (use with care) |

Always be explicit about which dispatcher you are running on:

| Dispatcher | Use for |
|---|---|
| `Dispatchers.Main` | UI updates, lightweight state changes |
| `Dispatchers.IO` | Network requests, disk reads/writes, database queries |
| `Dispatchers.Default` | CPU-intensive work: sorting, parsing, complex computation |
| `Dispatchers.Unconfined` | Testing only — do not use in production |

# Dependency Injection with Hilt

Hilt is the mandatory DI framework for all 57Blocks Android projects. It eliminates manual dependency wiring, enforces correct lifecycle scoping, and makes testing straightforward. Choosing the wrong scope is one of the most common sources of memory leaks in Android — this skill defines how to get it right.

## Scopes — The Most Critical Decision

Every binding in Hilt has a scope that determines its lifetime. Choosing too broad a scope causes memory leaks; too narrow causes unnecessary recreation.

| Scope | Annotation | Lifetime | Use for |
|---|---|---|---|
| Application | `@Singleton` | App process lifetime | Retrofit, OkHttpClient, Room DB, repositories |
| Activity | `@ActivityScoped` | Single Activity instance | Activity-level coordinators |
| ViewModel | `@ViewModelScoped` | ViewModel lifetime | UseCases, per-screen state holders |
| Fragment | `@FragmentScoped` | Single Fragment instance | Fragment-level helpers (rare) |
| None (unscoped) | — | New instance per injection | Simple stateless helpers |

[code block]

# Android Design Principles

## Material Design 3 Compliance

- **Default to Material 3 components** — use `MaterialTheme`, `MaterialButton`, `OutlinedTextField`, `NavigationBar` from `androidx.compose.material3` or `com.google.android.material`
- Do not override Material component behaviour without a documented design reason
- Navigation follows Material 3 conventions:
  - `NavigationBar` (bottom) for 3–5 top-level destinations on phones
  - `NavigationRail` for medium screens (tablets in portrait)
  - `NavigationDrawer` for large screens (tablets in landscape)
- Use `FloatingActionButton` only for the single primary action per screen — not for secondary actions

## Design-to-Engineering Handoff

- All designs must be in **Figma** with:
  - Exact spacing in `dp`
  - Named color styles mapping to `MaterialTheme.colorScheme.*` tokens
  - Named text styles mapping to `MaterialTheme.typography.*` styles
  - All interactive states: default, pressed, disabled, focused, loading, error
- A screen is **not ready for implementation** until all states are designed
- When a design spec is ambiguous or missing a state, block implementation until the...(truncated)

# Android Fragment Standards

Fragments have one of the most error-prone lifecycles in Android. These standards prevent the most common bugs: memory leaks from ViewBinding, incorrect communication patterns, and lifecycle misuse.

## Fragment Lifecycle — What Goes Where

Fragments have two lifecycles: the Fragment lifecycle and the **view** lifecycle. They are different and must be treated differently.

[code block]

| Method | What to do here |
|---|---|
| `onCreate` | Initialise non-view state; retrieve arguments via `arguments` |
| `onCreateView` | Inflate layout **only** — return the inflated view, nothing else |
| `onViewCreated` | Set up ViewBinding, observers, click listeners, RecyclerView adapters |
| `onStart` | Resume operations that need to be visible but not in foreground |
| `onResume` | Start operations that need full focus (camera, sensors) |
| `onPause` | Release focus-dependent resources |
| `onDestroyView` | **Null out `_binding`** — most critical step to prevent leaks |
| `onDestroy` | Release Fragment-level (non-view) resources |

[code block]
...(truncated)

# Jetpack Compose Standards

## Composable Design Principles

### State Hoisting
Always hoist state to the lowest common ancestor that needs it. Stateless Composables are easier to test, preview, and reuse:

[code block]

### Modifier as Last Parameter
Every Composable that renders UI must accept `modifier: Modifier = Modifier` as the last (or second-to-last if there are trailing lambdas) parameter, and apply it to the root element:

[code block]

### Single Responsibility
If a Composable's `@Preview` needs more than 3-4 parameters, it is doing too much. Extract sub-Composables.

## State Management

- Use `remember` for state that survives recomposition but not configuration changes
- Use `rememberSaveable` for state that must survive configuration changes (text input, scroll position)
- Use `collectAsStateWithLifecycle()` (not `collectAsState()`) to collect `StateFlow` — it respects Android lifecycle and stops collection in the background:
  [code block]
- Derive state instead of duplicating it — use `remember(key) { derivedStateOf { ... } }` for computed values

## Side Effects

| API | Us...(truncated)

# Kotlin Coding Standards

Follow these standards when writing Kotlin code across all 57Blocks Android projects.

## Naming Conventions

### Classes and Objects
- **PascalCase** for classes, objects, interfaces, enums, annotations
- Interface names describe capability or contract: `Authenticatable`, `UserRepository`, `NetworkDataSource`
- Sealed classes use PascalCase; subclasses are concise nouns or adjectives: `sealed class Result { data class Success(...); data class Error(...); object Loading }`

### Functions and Properties
- **camelCase** for functions, properties, local variables
- Boolean properties read as assertions: `isLoading`, `hasPermission`, `canRetry`

# Android Performance Standards

## Main Thread Protection

The main thread must only handle UI rendering and user interaction. Any blocking work on the main thread causes ANR (Application Not Responding) after 5 seconds.

- **Never perform network, disk I/O, or heavy computation on the main thread**
- Use `Dispatchers.IO` for I/O, `Dispatchers.Default` for CPU-intensive work
- Any single operation taking more than **16ms** risks dropping a frame at 60fps
- Monitor with **Android Studio Profiler → CPU → Main Thread** — the main thread must show no blocking calls

## App Startup Optimisation

- **Cold start target: under 1 second** to first frame on mid-range devices (Pixel 4a equivalent)
- Defer all non-critical initialisation out of `Application.onCreate()`:
  [code block]
- Use **Baseline Profiles** to pre-compile critical code paths at install time — this reduces cold start by up to 40%:
  [code block]
- Reduce the number of classes loaded at startup — each initialised class adds to startup time

## Compose Recomposition

- **Pass only what a Composable needs** — passing the entire `UiState` to a leaf Composable forc...(truncated)

Organise by **feature modules** with a shared `core` layer. Single-module apps become unmaintainable; over-modularisation adds build complexity with little benefit. The right threshold is one module per major feature for apps with 3+ distinct features.

- `app` depends on all feature modules — feature modules never depend on each other
- Feature modules depend on `core-*` modules only
- `core-domain` has zero Android framework dependencies — pure Kotlin

Never hardcode dependency versions in `build.gradle.kts` files — always reference `libs.*` from the version catalog.

### Build Configurations

Use three build types:
- `debug` — development, logging enabled, debuggable, no obfuscation
- `staging` — mirrors production config, connects to staging API, signed
- `release` — production, R8 enabled, logging disabled, signed

# Android Security Constraints

## Sensitive Data Storage

**Never store sensitive data in `SharedPreferences`** — it is stored as plaintext XML and readable on rooted devices:

[code block]

- Use **Android Keystore** for cryptographic key storage — keys never leave the secure hardware
- Store authentication tokens in `EncryptedSharedPreferences` or Keystore-backed storage
- For structured sensitive data, use **EncryptedFile** from Jetpack Security Crypto
- Never write sensitive data to logs — `Logcat` is readable by any app on a rooted device in debug builds

## Secrets and API Keys

- **Never hardcode API keys or secrets in source code** — they are extractable from APKs with `apktool`
- Inject secrets via `BuildConfig` from CI environment variables or `local.properties` (gitignored):
  [code block]
- For secrets that must be embedded, use Android Keystore to decrypt them at runtime — never store the raw secret
- All required secrets must be documented by name (not value) in `SECRETS.md` at the project root

## Network Security

# XML Views to Jetpack Compose Migration

Migration is always incremental. XML and Compose coexist throughout the process. Never attempt a big-bang rewrite — the project must ship normally during migration.

## Should You Migrate?

**Migrate when:**
- The app targets Android 5.0+ (API 21) and the team owns long-term maintenance
- New features are cleaner to build in Compose and existing screens are stable
- The team has allocated explicit capacity — do not bundle migration into feature sprints
- The codebase has reasonable test coverage on the ViewModel layer

**Do not migrate when:**
- The project ends within 6 months
- The existing XML screens are actively changing — migrating moving targets is costly
- The ViewModel layer has no tests — migration removes the safety net

## Migration Order: Leaf Components First

1. **Reusable components** — buttons, cards, list items, empty states
2. **Simple screens** — display-only screens with little interaction
3. **Form screens** — single-purpose input screens
4. **List screens** — after a stable Compose list item exists
5. ...(truncated)

## ViewBinding — Mandatory

## Fragment Lifecycle

Use each lifecycle method correctly:

| Method | Correct use |
|---|---|
| `onCreateView` | Inflate layout only — no logic |
| `onViewCreated` | Setup ViewBinding, observers, click listeners |
| `onStart` | Resume operations visible to user |
| `onResume` | Start foreground operations (camera, sensors) |
| `onPause` | Pause foreground operations |
| `onDestroyView` | Null out `_binding` to prevent leaks |
| `onDestroy` | Final cleanup |

**Never** start network requests directly in `onResume` — use ViewModel with `lifecycleScope` instead. `onResume` fires on every foreground entry, causing redundant requests.

### Always use ListAdapter with DiffUtil

- **Never call `notifyDataSetChanged()`** — it redraws everything and loses animations. Use `submitList()`.

# Component Rules

These rules define how UI components are built and structured across all 57Blocks iOS projects — covering both SwiftUI and UIKit. The goal is a clear separation between presentation and logic, predictable component APIs, and components that are easy to preview and test.

## View Responsibilities

Views are **pure presentation**. A View must not:
- Make network requests directly
- Write to a database or persistent storage
- Contain business logic or validation rules
- Hold state that belongs to the application domain

A View is responsible only for:
- Rendering the data it is given
- Forwarding user interactions to its ViewModel or parent via callbacks
- Managing ephemeral, purely visual state (e.g., animation state, hover state)

[code block]

## ViewModel Responsibilities

Each screen or significant UI section has exactly one ViewModel. The ViewModel:
- Is annotated with `@MainActor`
- Owns all state that the View renders
- Contains all business logic, validation, and navigation decisions
- Calls into Services; never into other ViewModels
- Is initializ...(truncated)

# Swift Concurrency Standards

Swift Concurrency is the mandatory approach for all asynchronous code in new 57Blocks iOS projects. These standards define correct usage patterns, prevent data races, and ensure safe migration toward Swift 6 strict concurrency.

## Foundational Rule

**Use Swift Structured Concurrency exclusively for new async code.** Do not introduce new completion handlers, `DispatchQueue`, or `NotificationCenter`-based async patterns. When working in legacy code that uses these patterns, migrate the call site to `async/await` at the boundary rather than propagating the old pattern deeper.

---

## async / await

- Mark a function `async` only when it actually suspends — a function that calls another `async` function is `async`; wrapping synchronous work in `async` for no reason is noise
- Use `try await` at the call site; do not wrap `async throws` calls in `Task { try? ... }` to silently discard errors
- `async` functions called from a `@MainActor` context run on the main actor unless they explicitly hop off — be explicit about which actor a function runs on when it matters:

[code b...(truncated)

# Dead Code Standards

Dead code is any code, asset, or dependency that is never executed, never referenced, or has no effect on the running app. It increases binary size, slows build times, confuses new engineers, and makes refactoring harder. Removing it is not optional housekeeping — it is part of shipping quality software.

## What Counts as Dead Code

- Unused classes, structs, enums, and protocols
- Unused functions and methods (including `private` and `internal` declarations)
- Unused properties and stored variables
- Unreachable `switch` cases or `if` branches (e.g., conditions that are always `true` or always `false`)
- Unused function parameters
- Commented-out code blocks left in the codebase
- Unused image assets, color assets, and localisation strings
- Unused SPM packages or CocoaPods dependencies
- Feature flags that have been fully rolled out or permanently disabled
- Deprecated internal APIs that have no remaining callers

## Tooling

# iOS Design Principles

These principles govern how engineering implements design on iOS. They are not aesthetic preferences — they are the baseline that ensures what ships matches what was designed and meets Apple's platform expectations.

## Apple Human Interface Guidelines Compliance

- **Default to HIG-native behavior** — do not override system gestures, navigation patterns, or interaction models without an explicit product reason documented in the design spec
- Use **SF Symbols** for all iconography where a suitable symbol exists — custom icons require explicit design review and must be delivered in all required weights and sizes
- Navigation follows platform conventions:
  - Push navigation via `NavigationStack` for hierarchical content
  - Modal sheets (`sheet`, `fullScreenCover`) for tasks the user needs to complete before returning
  - Tab bars for top-level app sections (maximum 5 tabs)
  - Do not invent custom navigation gestures that conflict with system swipe-back behavior
- Respect **system affordances**: tappable elements look tappable, destructive actions have visual weight appropriate to their consequence

## Design-to-Engineeri...(truncated)

# iOS Performance Standards

Performance is a feature. These standards define what to measure, what to avoid, and how to diagnose issues before they reach production.

## Main Thread Protection

The main thread is exclusively for UI rendering and user interaction. Any work that blocks it produces visible jank.

- **Never perform network requests, disk I/O, JSON parsing, image decoding, or heavy computation on the main thread**
- Use `async/await` with `@MainActor` to hop off and back correctly:
  [code block]
- Any synchronous operation taking more than **16ms** risks dropping a frame at 60fps, and more than **8ms** at 120fps (ProMotion) — keep the main thread clear of anything in this range
- Use Instruments → **Time Profiler** to identify main thread hotspots; look for call stacks where the main thread is blocked by work that has no UI dependency

## Memory Management

- **Audit retain cycles on every PR that introduces closures stored on `self`** — use `[weak self]` and confirm with Instruments → **Leaks** and **Allocations**
- Monitor `deinit` calls in ViewModels during deve...(truncated)

# iOS Project Structure

These conventions define how 57Blocks iOS projects are organized. A consistent structure reduces onboarding time, makes navigation predictable, and keeps feature boundaries explicit.

## Top-Level Folder Organization

Organize by **feature**, not by type. Each feature folder is self-contained and owns its Views, ViewModels, Models, and feature-specific services.

[code block]

## Feature Module Rules

- A feature module owns everything it needs — a feature should not reach into another feature's internal types
- Cross-feature dependencies go through `Shared/` or `Core/`, never direct feature-to-feature imports
- If a component is used by more than one feature, it belongs in `Shared/Components/`, not in either feature folder
- Feature folders mirror each other: every feature that has a ViewModel has a corresponding test file in `ProjectNameTests/`

## Naming Conventions for Files and Groups

- View files: `<FeatureName>View.swift`, `<FeatureName>Screen.swift` for top-level screens
- ViewModel files: `<FeatureName>ViewModel.swift`
- Service files: `<FeatureName>Service.swift`
- Model files: noun only, `User.swift`, `Order.swift`, `PaymentMethod.swift`
- ...(truncated)

---
description: "Apply 57Blocks iOS security constraints. Use when storing sensitive data, handling authentication tokens, configuring network security (ATS, certificate pinning), managing API keys and secrets, or reviewing code for security issues on a 57Blocks iOS project."

# Security Constraints

These constraints are non-negotiable across all 57Blocks iOS projects. Security failures in mobile apps affect client reputation and can expose end users to real harm.

## Sensitive Data Storage

- **Never store sensitive data in `UserDefaults`** — it is stored as a plaintext plist and readable without device encryption:
  [code block]
- Sensitive data includes: authentication tokens, session identifiers, passwords, PII (name, email, phone, address), payment information, and biometric-linked data
- Use the iOS **Keychain** with `kSecAttrAccessibleWhenUnlockedThisDeviceOnly` for most sensitive items — do not use `kSecAttrAccessibleAlways`
- Do not store sensitive data in plain files in the Documents or Caches directory — use Data Protection API (`NSFileProtectionComplete`)

## Secrets and API Keys

- **Never hardcode API keys, client secrets, or tokens in source code** — these are extracted trivially from app binaries
- Inject secrets at build time via CI environment variables into `.xcconfig` files; they are never committed to the repository
- All secrets used in the project must be documented by name (not value) ...(truncated)

# Swift Coding Standards

Follow these standards when writing Swift and SwiftUI code across all iOS projects at 57Blocks.

## Naming Conventions

### Types and Protocols
- Use **PascalCase** for all types: classes, structs, enums, protocols
- Protocol names should describe capability: `Authenticatable`, `NetworkRequestable`, `Persistable`
- Generic type parameters use single uppercase letters or descriptive PascalCase: `T`, `Element`, `ResponseType`

### Variables, Functions, and Properties
- Use **camelCase** for variables, functions, properties, and enum cases
- Names must be self-documenting — avoid abbreviations (`userIdentifier` not `usrId`)
- Boolean properties and functions read as assertions: `isLoading`, `hasPermission`, `canSubmit`
- Functions that perform actions use verb phrases: `fetchUserProfile()`, `validateInput()`, `dismissModal()`
- Avoid type names in variable names:
  [code block]

### Constants
- Use **enums without cases** for namespacing constants — never use global `let` constants:
  [code block]

### Files
- One primary type per file; file name matches the type name exactly
- Extensions in separate ...(truncated)
